title: House Robber
problem_statement: You are a professional robber planning to rob houses along a street. Each house has a certain amount of money stashed, the only constraint stopping you from robbing each of them is that adjacent houses have security systems connected and it will automatically contact the police if two adjacent houses were broken into on the same night. Given an integer array nums representing the amount of money of each house, return the maximum amount of money you can rob tonight without alerting the police.

examples.explanation: Rob house 1 (money = 1) and then rob house 3 (money = 3). Total amount you can rob = 1 + 3 = 4.
Rob house 1 (money = 2), rob house 3 (money = 9) and rob house 5 (money = 1). Total amount you can rob = 2 + 9 + 1 = 12.

intuition: For each house, decide whether to rob it (add its value to the max from two houses ago) or skip it (take the max from the previous house).

approaches.name: Recursive Brute Force
approaches.description: At each house, choose to rob it (add value, skip next) or skip it. Recurse on the rest.
approaches.why_not: Exponential due to exploring all robbery choices. Overlapping subproblems make it inefficient.
interview_commentary: This is like a decision tree: for each house, I can rob it or not. If I rob it, I can't rob the next one.
The recursion will explore all paths, but many subproblems overlap — for example, the max from index 2 is computed multiple times.
This works for small n but will be slow for n=100.

approaches.name: Memoized Recursion (Top-Down DP)
approaches.description: Same recursion, but memoize the result for each index to avoid recomputation.
approaches.why_not: Bottom-up is iterative and can be done in constant space.
interview_commentary: To optimize, I'll use memoization. memo[i] will store the max robbery starting from house i.
This brings time to O(n) since each index is computed once.
Space is O(n) for the memo array.

approaches.name: Bottom-Up Dynamic Programming
approaches.description: Use two variables to track the max robbery if we rob the previous house or not. Iterate through the array updating these values.
approaches.why_not: This is optimal for space and time.
interview_commentary: Bottom-up: prev is the max without robbing current, curr is the max up to previous.
For each house, next = max(curr, prev + num) — curr is skip, prev + num is rob.
Then shift prev = curr, curr = next.
This uses O(1) space by only keeping the last two states.

interview_qa.a: rob(i) = max(rob(i-1), rob(i-2) + nums[i]). rob(i-1) means skipping house i, rob(i-2) + nums[i] means robbing house i.

interview_qa.q: Why can't we rob adjacent houses?
interview_qa.a: Because they have connected security systems that would alert the police.

interview_qa.q: How does this relate to maximum sum with no two adjacent elements?
interview_qa.a: Exactly — it's the same problem. The DP captures that constraint.

interview_qa.q: What if the array is empty?
interview_qa.a: Return 0, as there are no houses to rob.
